text: The structure and electronic properties of reaction centers determine the performance of catalysts. The centers usually undergo unstable structural changes in the operational process, which is not easily controlled. This work reveals a method of readily activating light to regulate the unstable structural transformation of the active sites of catalysts (Cu species on the TiO2 support catalysts) in low temperature (150 °C) methanol steam reforming. The activity and stability of catalysts can be notably enhanced by up-shifting the structure of catalysts from thermally inactive Cu2O to their highly active metallic Cu states using charge carriers generated from the TiO2 support triggered by the light-activation. Under low light intensity, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst yields a remarkably high rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹ H2 at least one order of magnitude higher than most of the reported photocatalysts and thermocatalysts. It reveals the strong coupling between light, matter and reactants, providing a convenient and timely strategy to modulate the active centers of the catalyst in situ, which is conducive to in-situ catalyst functionalization and maintenance.
